article.label: Published Article
article.title: RPKI Ghost: the invisible risk in route validation
article.description: By Carlos Pérez / NIC - CRIX Costa Rica

articleDetail.label: RPKI Security
articleDetail.author: By Carlos Pérez / NIC - CRIX Costa Rica
articleDetail.title_part2: the invisible risk in route validation

content.p1: Nowadays, in an environment where AI and best practice manuals are everywhere, even following community standards can lead us into a dangerous trap: assuming that RPKI security just works.
content.p2: Added to this is the classic 'if it works, don't touch it' mentality, very common among operators and administrators. Although it may seem far-fetched, it is entirely plausible that ISPs —and even IXPs— believe they are protected simply because their RPKI validator is on, with no apparent errors and no visible alerts.
content.quote1: An outdated validator can become a 'Ghost': it looks like it's working, but it's not actually validating.
content.p3: Many operators configured their validators years ago, when RSYNC (RFC 9286) was the main synchronization method. However, RIRs have since moved towards RRDP (RFC 8210), an HTTPS-based mechanism that is much faster, more efficient, and less costly for repositories.
content.p_sub: What if your validator stopped validating years ago... but you never noticed because the RTR session remained 'Established'?
content.ghost_def: That is the heart of Ghost RPKI: An apparently healthy validator, but with broken validation and no visible symptoms.
content.h3_1: Real Comparison: FORT v1.0 vs FORT v1.6
content.p4: The following illustrates massive differences in the number of imported and preferred ROAs between an old validator and an updated one.
content.fig1: Figure 1 – Real BIRD output showing differences between old and updated validators.
content.fig2: Figure 2 – Comparison of the number of ROAs imported by FORT v1.0 and v1.6 (IPv4 and IPv6).
content.fig2_cap: The old version loses a large amount of data due to incomplete RRDP support.
content.fig3: Figure 3 – Comparison between imported ROAs and preferred ROAs. FORT v1.0 shows strong inconsistencies revealing degraded validation (Ghost RPKI).
list: Duplication or incorrect processing of objects
Maintaining inconsistent states by relying only on RSYNC
Failure to recognize objects introduced after 2021
Creating a false sense of security (Ghost Security)
content.conclusion_p: In simple terms, 'Ghost RPKI' is a validator that seems to work but is actually protecting with incomplete, corrupt, or old data. False security is worse than having no RPKI. The true ghost is thinking you are protected when you are not.
recommendations: Don't trust that 'it shows no errors'
Don't trust 'it has always worked this way'
RPKI is not an 'install and forget' service
Keep your validator, TALs, and repositories updated
content.final_quote: The biggest risk is not having no RPKI: it is believing you have it, when in reality your validator is a ghost.

projects.title: Projects
projects.subtitle: Some of the projects I have worked on. All available on GitHub.

items.title: zabbix-bgp-monitoring-bird
items.description: Automated BGP monitoring on BIRD routers (IXP – NIC CR) using Zabbix Agent, scripts, and LLD (IPv4/IPv6).

items.title: zabbix-bgp-ipv4-ipv6-monitoring
items.description: Zabbix template to monitor BGP IPv4/IPv6 sessions on Cisco routers via SNMPv2.

items.title: nettools-pro
items.description: Collection of tools and Docker containers developed for network testing, validation, and automation (CRIXTools).
